coredata.dc:title: Optimization of titanium dioxide decorated by graphene quantum dot as a light scatterer for enhanced dye-sensitized solar cell performance
coredata.dc:creator: Mustafa, Muhammad Norhaffis
Sulaiman, Yusran
coredata.dc:description: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) as a photoanode in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) has some drawbacks that reduce its photovoltaic performances i.e. low dye loading capacity and low light-harvesting efficiency. Therefore, TiO2 decorated by graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a light scatterer has been successfully fabricated via electrodeposition and drop-casting. The response surface methodology/central composite design was successfully utilized to optimize the preparation of photoanode with TiO2-GQD as a light scattering layer (LSL). A reduced quadratic model was successfully designed to predict the power conversion efficiency (PCE) accurately up to 97% with a 3% residual standard error. The TiO2-GQD LSL depicted a cluster of spherical nanoparticles on top of the photoanode that not only enhanced the light scattering effect but also improved the light-harvesting range from visible light to ultraviolet and near-infrared range. The resultant TiO2 nanoparticles with TiO2-GQD LSL showed vast enhancement of PCE up to 66% from 3.06% to 5.01% due to a good synergistic effect.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Experimental design and RSM for the preparation of TiO2-GQD as an LSL
Statistical analysis and regression equation development
PL analysis and photovoltaic performance of DSSC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration for the light loss of (a) TNs, (b) TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL and (c) schematic diagram of the complete DSSC device.
Plot of (a) PCE against the concentration of TTIP, (b) PCE against the number of cycles and (c) PCE versus volume of GQD.
3D surface plot of (a) PCE versus the concentration of TTIP and number of cycles and (b) PCE versus the concentration of TTIP and volume of GQD.
(a) FTIR spectra and (b) XRD pattern of TNs, GQD, TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. Peaks marked with “♦” and “♣” refer to the GQD and TiO2, respectively.

FESEM images of (a) TNs, (b) TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL, (c) cross-section of photoanode with TiO2-GQD LSL and (d) EDX spectrum of photoanode with TiO2-GQD LSL. Inset is the table of each element and atomic percentage in the TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL.

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL at different excitation wavelengths (b) PL spectra of TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL and TNs with TiO2 LSL at an excitation wavelength 280 nm, (c) Dark J-V curves and (d) J-V curves of TNs and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL under the illumination of light.
(a) Nyquist plots, (b) bode plot, (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra and (d) diffuse reflectance spectra of TNs, TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. Inset is the equivalent circuit for EIS.
Experimental design with actual and predicted results.
ANOVA of the PCE for the reduced quadratic model.
Constraints of each independent parameters and surface response for optimization.
Predicted and experiment values conducted at optimum conditions.
DSSC performances of TNs, TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL and comparison with other literature.

row.entry: TiO2 + TiO2 nanowires with nanoscale whiskers

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impedance data of the DSSC based on TNs, TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL.

head.ce:title: Optimization of titanium dioxide decorated by graphene quantum dot as a light scatterer for enhanced dye-sensitized solar cell performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Functional Devices Laboratory, Institute of Advanced Technology, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, UPM, Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400, Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, Universiti Putra Malaysia, 43400

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) as a photoanode in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) has some drawbacks that reduce its photovoltaic performances i.e. low dye loading capacity and low light-harvesting efficiency. Therefore, TiO2 decorated by graphene quantum dot (GQD) as a light scatterer has been successfully fabricated via electrodeposition and drop-casting. The response surface methodology/central composite design was successfully utilized to optimize the preparation of photoanode with TiO2-GQD as a light scattering layer (LSL). A reduced quadratic model was successfully designed to predict the power conversion efficiency (PCE) accurately up to 97% with a 3% residual standard error. The TiO2-GQD LSL depicted a cluster of spherical nanoparticles on top of the photoanode that not only enhanced the light scattering effect but also improved the light-harvesting range from visible light to ultraviolet and near-infrared range. The resultant TiO2 nanoparticles with TiO2-GQD LSL showed vast enhancement of PCE up to 66% from 3.06% to 5.01% due to a good synergistic effect.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past twenty years, dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) have been extensively studied because of their positive traits such as inexpensive, facile fabrication processes and high efficiency [1–3]. Nowadays, the highest PCE of DSSCs recorded has reached up to 14% under one sun illumination using titanium dioxide (TiO2) film as a photoanode [4]. The DSSCs process starts when the photon from the sunlight strikes the dye molecules and the dye molecules undergo oxidation to release an electron. The electrons are excited from the ground state to the excited state and transfer to the photoanode and combine with redox electrolyte before recombining with the dye molecules [5]. During the DSSC process, a photocurrent and voltage are generated to produce solar energy. Despite being extensively studied, one of the most crucial components in DSSC which is photoanode typically TiO2 possesses a major drawback of low light scattering ability because of the small structure of TiO2 nanoparticles (TNs) [6]. Therefore, a light scattering layer (LSL) was introduced to overcome this problem. The theory of LSL was introduced in early 1997 by Usami [7] where the scattering effect was increased due to an increase in the optical absorption that leads to high PCE upon addition of larger TNs on top of the TiO2 photoanode. The diagram of the light loss on the photoanode without LSL and with LSL is shown in Fig. 1a and b while Fig. 1c displays the schematic diagram of the DSSC. The TNs with TiO2 LSL reduce the light loss and enhance the utilization of the incident light to excite more electrons and produce more photocurrent resulting in high PCE.

A large diameter of TiO2 (200–400 nm) with numerous morphologies such as microspheres [8], pop-corn like structure [9], nanorods [10], nanotubes [11], hollow box [12] and hydrangea-like structure [13] have been widely utilized as LSL to increase the DSSC performance. Chen, et al. [9] fabricated a pop-corn like TiO2 as LSL by using a simple solution process to increase the effective charge transfer path and surface area. The DSSC performance with pop-corn like TiO2 as LSL shows an increment up to 8.5% compared to the bare photoanode [9]. He, et al. [12] used a solid-state precursor technique to prepare a hollow box TiO2 as LSL that led to a prolonged electron lifetime (τn) that can enhance light scattering ability. The result also displays an enhancement of PCE up to 101% compared to the bare photoanode. Besides, TiO2 nanotubes array as LSL was successfully synthesized by Liu, et al. [11] using potentiostatic anodization and the PCE improved from 5.18% to 6.15% due to high scattering capability.

Graphene quantum dot (GQD) possesses a high surface area to volume ratio [14] and photoluminescent effect [15] that is capable to enhance the PCE as a result of the increase in dye loading capacity and enhance light harvesting range from visible light to ultraviolet and near-infrared range. Fang, et al. [15] fabricated GQD modified TiO2 photoanode using the dip-coating method and the PCE increased from 5.1% to 6.1% upon addition of GQD due to photoluminescent effect, while Mohamed Sharif, et al. [16] reported that the DSSC performance increased up to 70% upon the addition of GQD into the photoanode. This vast improvement is due to the photoluminescent effect of GQD that can generate more electron-hole pairs that lead to high PCE [16].

Response surface methodology (RSM) is a well-known optimization method to study the interaction between two or more parameters because RSM can lessen the number of experimental runs while maintaining sufficient information on statistical and regression analysis [17]. In addition, the developed model by RSM can identify the significance of each parameter towards the surface response, thereby reducing the labor work and minimizing the experimental cost [18]. In this work, facile electrodeposition assisted drop-casting techniques were applied to fabricate TiO2-GQD as LSL. The optimization of the TiO2-GQD LSL was carried out using RSM and central composite design. A reduced quadratic model displayed a low residual standard error (RSE) indicating good model predictability. Upon the addition of TiO2-GQD as LSL, the DSSC performance increased from 3.06% to 5.10%. The enhanced DSSC performance of TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL was attributed to the good synergistic effect possessed by the LSL which was high dye loading capacity and better light scattering ability. The TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL also exhibited a longer τn, higher charge collection efficiency (ηc) and higher light reflectance, demonstrating good light scattering material.

GQD was obtained from ACS material, while nanocrystalline cellulose (NCC) was acquired from the University of Maine. Titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP), 3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT), polyvinyl-alcohol (PVA) and TiO2 (Degussa) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Ethanol, tert-butanol and acetone were acquired from J.Kollin Chemicals, Bendosen and QReC®, respectively. Potassium chloride (KCl) was obtained from Fisher Scientific. Acetonitrile and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were attained from Friendemann Schmidt. Iodolyte Z − 100 and di-tetrabutylammonium cis-bis(isothiocyanato)bis(2,2′-bipyridyl-4,4′-dicarboxylato)ruthenium(II) (N719) were obtained from Solaronix SA. Throughout the experiments, all the solutions were prepared using deionized water (DI Milli-Q 18.2 MΩ.cm).

The TiO2 compact layer was fabricated using electrospinning. Briefly, the electrospun solution was prepared by dissolving 6.32 wt% PVA in DI water. The PVA solution was stirred until a clear homogenous solution was formed at 80 °C. Then, the mixture was stirred for 2 h after adding 0.15 mL TTIP [19]. A doctor blade technique was applied to fabricate the photoanodes. Concisely, the TiO2 paste was prepared by mixing 2 g of TiO2 nanopowder (Degussa P25) with 8 mL of ethanol and stirred for 2 h. 0.16 mL TTIP was then added and the paste was sonicated for 30 min in an ultrasonic bath [20]. The chronoamperometry method was used to fabricate the PEDOT-NCC counter electrode. In a brief, NCC (1 mg/mL) was mixed with EDOT (10 mM) and deposited on ITO at a constant applied voltage of 1.2 V and deposition time of 100 s [21]. The photoanodes were undergone a dye bath process to produce sensitized photoanodes in a solution of N719 (0.2 mM) dissolved in acetonitrile:tert-butanol (1:1) for 24 h [22,23]. The DSSCs were assembled using a two-electrode system by sandwiching the counter electrode and the sensitized photoanode. The redox electrolyte was injected in between the counter electrode and sensitized photoanode before the efficiencies of the DSSCs were measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design and RSM for the preparation of TiO2-GQD as an LSL
ce:section.ce:para:
The RSM based central composite design (CCD) was selected to execute the statistical analysis. The Design Expert 6.0 software was used as a statistical expert. Three independent parameters that have been determined using the preliminary experiments were optimized to increase the surface response (Table 1 ). The TiO2-GQD LSL was fabricated using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and drop-casting technique. The TiO2 LSL was deposited on top of the photoanode via a three-electrode system in an aqueous solution comprising different concentrations of TTIP in the range of 50 to 150 mM and 0.1 M KCl. The reference, counter and working electrodes were Ag/Ag+, platinum wire and ITO/compact layer/TNs, respectively. The potential applied was in the range of 0 to −1.5 V with a scan rate of 25 mVs−1 and the number of cycles used was in the range of 1 to 3. A different volume of GQD (1–10 μL) was drop casted on top of the TiO2 LSL to form TiO2-GQD LSL. The CCD model was designed to fit the second-order polynomial model (Eq. 1).
(1)
y =
β o
+
∑
i = 1
k
β i
χ i
+
∑
i = 1
k
β ii
χ i 2
+
∑
i = 1
k − 1
∑
j > i
k
β ij
χ i
χ j
+ ε
where y signifies predicted responses, both χ
i and χ
j represent factors and Ԑ is the residual term. β 0, β
i , β
ii, and β
ij are the regression coefficients of variables for the intercept, linear, quadratic and interaction terms, respectively [24,25].

Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR, Perkin-Elmer, USA) spectrometer was used to study the functional groups of LSL. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using the Shimadzu XRD Diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54 Ǻ). Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, JEOL JSM-7600F) analyses were performed to determine the morphological study of the LSL. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out to study the charge transfer resistance (R ct) and series resistance (R s). The EIS analysis was performed using a two-electrode system with an open circuit potential of 0.8 V and an AC amplitude of 5 mV in the dark condition with the presence of redox electrolyte. The frequency range was set from 100 kHz to 1 Hz. The dye loading capacity was performed by desorbing the sensitized photoanode in 0.1 M NaOH. The Autolab Spectrophotometer UA was used to perform the ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption and diffused reflectance analysis in the range of 200–550 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were acquired by Perkin Elmer, LS 55 fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm with a slit interval of 8 nm. The solar simulator (Oriel LCS-100) was used to measure the current-voltage (J-V) curves under 1.5 A.M. (100 mWcm−2). All the electrochemical measurements were performed using a potentiostat (Autolab PGSTAT202) equipped with NOVA software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis and regression equation development
ce:section.ce:para: RSM was carried out to study the optimum condition of each independent factor towards the surface response. Illustration of the CCD for photoanode with TiO2-GQD LSL is shown in Table 2 . The predicted PCE values were generated by Design-Expert software based on the experimental data and the suggested model generated twenty sets of experiments. The ANOVA of PCE for the reduced quadratic model is shown in Table 3 and the results show a highly significant reduced quadratic model because the prob. > F value is <0.0001. The reduced quadratic model depicts the F-value of 88.09 and prob. > F < 0.05. In this reduced quadratic model, all the model terms are significant with prob. > F less than 0.05 except model term B. Moreover, the reduced quadratic model possesses an insignificant lack of fit (0.39) and a small standard deviation (0.16), demonstrating good model predictability and the data are close to the mean value. The reduced quadratic model also displays a high coefficient of determination (R2), high predicted R2 and high adjusted R2. The high R2 (0.9846) closer to 1 is favorable, indicating the model has a good regression while the predicted R2 is in reasonable agreement with the adjusted R2. This reduced quadratic model has an adequate precision of 27.701 which implies an adequate signal because an adequate precision greater than 4 is desirable for good model predictability.
The effect of the experimental independent and dependent variables on the PCE is shown in eq. 2. The positive and negative signs will increase and decrease the PCE, respectively.
where A, B and C are the concentration of TTIP, number of cycle and volume of GQD, respectively.

The FTIR and XRD analyses were performed to study the functional groups present in the photoanode and phase identification for crystalline material, respectively. Fig. 5a shows the FTIR spectra of TNs, GQD, TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. The band at 731 cm−1 is ascribed to the Ti-O-Ti stretching vibration while the bands at 3426 and 1647 cm−1 are assigned to the OH stretching mode and OH bending vibration of TiO2, respectively due to the adsorption of water on the surface of TiO2 [26–28]. The FTIR spectrum of GQD depicts the bands at 1423, 1676 and 3230 cm−1 that are attributed to the CC, CO stretching and OH stretching of GQD, respectively [29–31]. The FTIR spectrum of TiO2 LSL displays all the peaks of TNs, while the FTIR spectrum of TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL exhibits all the peaks of TNs with additional of GQD peaks. Fig. 5b depicts the XRD patterns of TNs, GQD, TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. The XRD diffractogram of TNs displays characteristic peaks at 25.6° A(101), 27.7° R(110), 36.3° R(101), 38.0° R(200), 41.4° R(111), 48.2° A(200), 54.2° A(211) and 55.3° R(220) which are attributed to the crystal planes of anatase and rutile TiO2 (JCPDS file No-21-1272). The XRD diffractogram of TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL shows all the XRD patterns of TNs with an additional characteristic peak at 22.6° (002) which is associated with the GQD [32,33]. In addition, the presence of the characteristic peaks at 31.0° B(121) and 58.3° B(123) features to the crystal planes of brookite TiO2 (JCPDS file No-29-1360) [34,35]. The presence of these additional peaks is due to the electrodeposition of TiO2 LSL at low temperatures that increase the crystal of brookite TiO2 [36].

The FESEM analysis was carried out to observe the morphology of the photoanode (Fig. 6 ) while the energy-dispersive x-ray analysis was executed as a conformational analysis to detect the presence of each element in the TiO2-GQD LSL (Fig. 6d). As shown in Fig. 6a, the TNs depict the porous spherical nanoparticles morphology which is crucial in the absorption of dye molecules [37,38]. Upon the addition of TiO2-GQD as LSL, a cluster of spherical nanoparticles is formed on top of the photoanode (Fig. 6b). This morphology is important to increase the photoanode light scattering ability due to the larger size of TiO2-GQD LSL increases the light scattering effect [7]. Fig. 6c displays the cross-sectional image of the photoanode with TiO2-GQD LSL and three elements present in the TiO2-GQD LSL which are carbon (7.1%), titanium (64.3%) and oxygen (28.6%) demonstrating the TiO2-GQD LSL was successfully deposited (Fig. 6d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL analysis and photovoltaic performance of DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of photoanodes were studied by performing the PL analysis. Fig. 7a and b display the PL spectra of TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. Fig. 8a shows the PL spectra of TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL at different excitation wavelengths in the range of 280–312 nm with the excitation slit of 8 nm. Two prominent PL emission peaks are observed at ~410 nm and ~ 470 nm when excited in the range of 280–312 nm. As the excitation wavelength increase, the PL emission decrease in intensity and shift to lower wavelength range (blue shifted), indicating the excitation-dependent PL behavior of the GQD that attribute to the various size distribution and the presence of impurity states of GQD [39]. Fig. 7b shows the PL spectra of TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. The result shows that the TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL display a higher PL emission peaks at around 420 nm compared to the TNs with TiO2 LSL. This indicates that the introduction of GQD as LSL may induce some new defect states that served as trapped states that preventing the recombination of electron-hole pair resulting in more photocurrent generated and high PCE [40].

Fig. 7c shows the dark J-V curves of TNs and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. Upon incorporation of TiO2-GQD LSL, the onset of the dark current increase from 0.58 V to 0.65 V, indicating reduces in back electron transfer. In order to study the DSSC performances, the TNs and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL were tested using J-V curves analysis under one sun illumination (Fig. 7d). TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL displays a PCE of 5.01% compared to the TNs (3.01%). A vast PCE increment up to 66% occurs upon the addition of TiO2-GQD as LSLdue to the presence of TiO2-GQD LSL that decreases the radiation loss and enhances the light scattering effect [41]. Furthermore, the introduction of TiO2-GQD LSL can also increase the length of the optical path and increase the utilization of incident light [42]. The extra light trapped by the TiO2-GQD LSL excites more dye molecules and produces more photocurrent that leads to high PCE. In addition, the unique properties of GQD that possess photoluminescent effect [15] and high surface area TiO2 [43] create a synergistic effect that enhances the PCE. The summary of the DSSC performances of the TNs and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL are listed in Table 6 and the PCE of the TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL is also comparable with reported literature.

The EIS analysis was conducted to compare the R ct, ηc and τn between the TNs, TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. The semicircle at high and low frequency is attributed to the charge transfer resistance at the counter electrode interface (R ct1) and the charge transfer resistance at the TiO2/dye/electrolyte interface (R ct2), respectively (Fig. 8a) [48–50]. The TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL depict a bigger R ct1 and R ct2 compared to both TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL and TNs (Table 7 ). This is because the introduction of the TiO2-GQD as LSL will hinder the electron flow to complete the electron-regeneration process [51] and increase the internal resistance [41,52]. The series resistance (R s) of the TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL follows the same trend as R ct1 and R ct2 (Table 7), indicating that incorporation of TiO2-GQD LSL increases the overall resistance of the photoanode. The inset of Fig. 8a is the electrical equivalent circuit for the fitted EIS data. The equivalent circuit is made of R s, R ct1, R ct2 and constant phase element (CPE) where the CPE signposts the inhomogeneity of the photoanode [53,54]. The TNs, TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL depict low chi-squares which are 0.00187, 0.00163 and 0.00832, respectively demonstrating good fitting between the Nyquist plot and the equivalent circuit.

Furthermore, ηc can also be extracted from the EIS analysis using eq. 3 [55].
(3)
η c
=
1 +
R s
R
ct 2
− 1

TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL exhibits a higher ηc (51.88%) compared to TNs with TiO2 LSL (46.43%) and the TNs (39.23%), demonstrating enhance light scattering effect for the former compared to the latter. The Bode plot analysis can also be extracted from EIS analysis to study the τn between TNs, TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL (Fig. 8b). The τn value can be calculated using eq. 4 [55]:
(4)
τ n
=
1
2 π
f max
where f max is the maximum frequency. As shown in Fig. 7b, the f max value is blue shifted after the TiO2-GQD LSL was incorporated on top of TNs. TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL exhibit a longer τn (39.99 ms) compared to the TNs with TiO2 LSL (25.22 ms) and TNs (7.98 ms) demonstrating a suppress recombination phenomenon that leads to high PCE [16].

The UV–Vis analysis was executed to study the effect of LSL on dye loading capacity. Fig. 8c shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of TNs and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL. The absorption bands at lower wavelengths (230 and 307 nm) and higher wavelengths (375 and 505 nm) are ascribed to the ligand-centered charge transfer transitions (π – π*) and metal-to-ligand charge transfer transition (4d – π*), respectively [56,57]. As shown in Fig. 8c, the TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL depict higher absorption peaks compared to TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs. This is because the presence of TiO2-GQD as LSL helps to increase the dye loading capacity due to more dye that can be absorbed by the porous nanoparticles of TiO2-GQD LSL. The number of dye molecules adsorbed by the TNs, TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL are 0.66 × 10−6, 0.67 × 10−6 and 0.72 × 10−6 mol.cm−3, respectively. The diffuse reflectance spectra were measured in order to study the light-scattering capability of each photoanode (Fig. 8d). The TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL display a higher light reflectance in the visible light regions (400–700 nm) compared to TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs. This can be attributed to the unique structure of TiO2-GQD LSL with a cluster of spherical nanoparticles morphology (Fig. 6b) which increases the light scattering effect [7]. In addition, TiO2-GQD LSL also depicts higher light reflectance in the ultraviolet regions (200–400 nm) and near-infrared regions (700–800 nm) compared to TNs with TiO2 LSL and TNs due to the presence of GQD that possesses the photoluminescence properties [58].

A facile approach was employed to produce TiO2 decorated by GQD as a light scattering layer via electrodeposition assisted drop-casting technique. The TiO2-GQD LSL was successfully optimized and fabricated using RSM/CCD. The reduced quadratic model displays a low RSE with 97% accuracy. The TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL show an enhanced PCE up to 66% compared to the bare photoanode. The TNs with TiO2-GQD LSL also exhibited a longer τn, higher ηc, higher dye loading capacity and higher light reflectance compared to the TNs with TiO2 LSL and bare photoanode demonstrating a good light scattering material.
The financial support from Universiti Putra Malaysia Research Grant (UPM/800-3/3/1/GPB/2018/9659200) is highly appreciated by the authors.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.
Author Contribution Statement.
Muhammad Norhaffis Mustafa: Investigation, Data Curation, Formal Analysis, Wirting -Original draft.
Yusran Sulaiman: Conceptualization, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision, Project Administration, Funding Acquisition.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dye-sensitized solar cells
Chem. Rev.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly-efficient dye-sensitized solar cells with collaborative sensitization by silyl-anchor and carboxy-anchor dyes
Chem. Commun.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in sensitized mesoscopic solar cells
Acc. Chem. Res.
